does: This paper introduces BigCodeArena, a human evaluation platform for code generation that extends Chatbot Arena by integrating live code execution capabilities. The system allows human evaluators to run LLM-generated code across multiple programming languages and environments, then interact with execution results to assess output quality. The authors collected over 14,000 conversation sessions from 10 major LLMs across 10 programming languages and 8 execution environments, yielding 4,700+ multi-turn preference samples. Through systematic analysis, they identify previously unexplored preferences and patterns in how humans evaluate code across fine-grained domains including specific tasks, languages, and frameworks, demonstrating that execution-informed evaluation reveals more reliable and nuanced judgments than manual code review alone.
connects: This work builds on crowdsourced evaluation platforms like Chatbot Arena and LMSYS Arena, which pioneered real-time human preference collection for LLM outputs. It responds to a critical challenge in the code generation literature: the difficulty of reliably evaluating generated code without executing it, which has limited prior preference studies to summary-based or test-case-based metrics. The paper extends recent work on LLM evaluation for code by integrating execution as a core evaluation component, similar to how code benchmarks like HumanEval and MBPP use test execution, but within an interactive human preference framework. It also connects to broader efforts in understanding model behavior across different domains and the role of human feedback in model assessment.
enables: BigCodeArena establishes infrastructure for training code-specific preference models and reward functions grounded in execution-informed human judgments, enabling more accurate fine-tuning of code generation models. The dataset of 4,700+ preference pairs across diverse languages and environments creates a resource for developing specialized reward models for code tasks. This work points toward automating execution-based evaluation for code, where models learn to predict which generated code will execute correctly and accomplish user intent. Future work can leverage these execution-informed preferences to improve reinforcement learning from human feedback (RLHF) for coding tasks, develop better automatic code evaluation metrics, and build specialized evaluators that understand the relationship between code structure, execution behavior, and user satisfaction across different programming domains.
